content:
# Titus Chapter 1 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), page 222-223 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/TIT_001_page_001.jpg, TIT_001_page_002.jpg

1. Paul, a servant of God and an apostle of Jesus Christ, according to the faith of the elect of God and the knowledge of the truth that accords with godliness,

2. in hope of eternal life, which God, who does not lie, promised before the ages of time,

3. but at the proper time manifested his word in the proclamation with which I was entrusted by the command of God our Savior;

4. To Titus, my true child according to a common faith: Grace and peace from God the Father and Christ Jesus our Savior.

5. For this reason I left you in Crete, that you might set in order the things that remain and appoint elders in every city, as I directed you —

6. if anyone is above reproach, the husband of one wife, having faithful children not under accusation of profligacy or insubordination.

7. For the overseer must be above reproach as God's steward, not self-willed, not quick-tempered, not given to wine, not violent, not greedy for dishonest gain,

8. but hospitable, a lover of good, self-controlled, upright, holy, disciplined,

9. holding fast the faithful word according to the teaching, so that he may be able both to exhort in sound doctrine and to rebuke those who contradict it.

10. For there are many who are insubordinate, empty talkers and deceivers, especially those of the circumcision,

11. who must be silenced, who overturn whole households, teaching things which they ought not, for the sake of dishonest gain.

12. One of them, a prophet of their own, said, "Cretans are always liars, evil beasts, idle gluttons."

13. This testimony is true. For this cause rebuke them sharply, that they may be sound in the faith,

14. not giving heed to Jewish myths and commandments of men who turn away from the truth.

15. To the pure all things are pure, but to the defiled and unbelieving nothing is pure, but both their mind and their conscience are defiled.

16. They profess to know God, but by their works they deny him, being detestable and disobedient and unfit for any good work.

---
## LOGOS Translation Notes

1. **v.1 — "servant" (doulos):** Sinaiticus reads ΠΑΥΛΟΣ ΔΟΥΛΟΣ ΘΕΟΥ — "Paul, a servant of God." This is the only Pauline letter opening where Paul identifies himself as doulos theou ("servant of God") rather than doulos Christou Iesou. The term doulos carries the weight of total bondage-service; "servant" is retained as the formal equivalent while acknowledging its inadequacy to convey the full force of the Greek.

2. **v.2 — "who does not lie" (apseudes):** The Sinaiticus text reads ὁ ἀψευδὴς θεός — rendered "God, who does not lie." This is a hapax legomenon in the New Testament. The adjective apseudes ("free from falsehood") is rendered as a relative clause to preserve its adjectival force in natural English.

3. **v.5 — "set in order the things that remain" (epidiorthose ta leiponta):** The verb epidiorthoo appears only here in the New Testament. It carries the sense of further correcting or setting right what is still deficient — a continuation of work Paul had begun.

4. **v.6 — "faithful children" vs. "believing children":** Sinaiticus reads ΤΕΚΝΑ ΕΧΩΝ ΠΙΣΤΑ — tekna echon pista. The adjective pistos can mean either "faithful" or "believing." In this context of household qualifications, "faithful" is preferred as the broader term encompassing both loyalty and faith.

5. **v.7 — "overseer" (episkopos):** The shift from presbyteros (v.5) to episkopos (v.7) within the same passage indicates these terms were functionally interchangeable in the early Pauline communities. Both are rendered with their distinct English equivalents to preserve the textual distinction.

6. **v.12 — The Epimenides quotation:** "Cretans are always liars, evil beasts, idle gluttons" — attributed to Epimenides of Knossos (6th c. BCE). Paul calls this pagan poet "a prophet of their own" (idios auton prophetes), acknowledging a measure of truth even in non-scriptural sources.

7. **v.15 — "To the pure all things are pure":** A proverbial saying (cf. Rom 14:20) here deployed against the ascetic tendencies of the false teachers. The Sinaiticus text reads ΠΑΝΤΑ ΚΑΘΑΡΑ ΤΟΙΣ ΚΑΘΑΡΟΙΣ — the formal parallelism is preserved in translation.

8. **Christological title — "our Savior":** In Titus, the title soter ("Savior") is applied both to God (v.3) and to Christ Jesus (v.4). This dual application is a distinctive feature of the Pastoral Epistles and is preserved without harmonization.

— LOGOS, AI Translator
